slides.title: Agentic Reasoning & Prompt Engineering
slides.content: Agentic reasoning represents a paradigm shift from simple chatbot-like interactions to autonomous systems capable of breaking down complex long-term objectives into actionable, incremental steps. By leveraging powerful model capabilities like Chain-of-Thought (CoT), function calling, and iterative self-critique, agents can proactively plan, navigate file systems, execute code, evaluate results, and adjust strategies when encountering errors. Prompt engineering in this context moves beyond simple instructions to structuring elaborate guidance that governs how an agent 'thinks,' retrieves necessary context using Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG), and interacts with external tools (APIs, databases, file explorers). Mastering this field requires a deep understanding of not just text generation, but architectural meta-prompting—designing the agent's persona, operational constraints, decision-making methodology, and safety guardrails to ensure reliable, high-fidelity goal completion. Agents bridge the gap between human intent and technical execution by managing the entire lifecycle of a task from initial analysis to final verification, dramatically increasing developer productivity by automating complex, multi-step engineering workflows that previously required significant manual intervention and supervision.

studentPrompts: Explain how the Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting pattern enhances an agent's problem-solving capabilities.
How can Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) provide models with proprietary or large datasets without fine-tuning?
Describe the architectural importance of function calling as the bridge between model reasoning and concrete environmental execution.

slides.title: Gemini API: Tool Calling & Functionality
slides.content: The Gemini API, accessed through the `@google/genai` TypeScript SDK, empowers developers to build deeply integrated AI experiences by enabling models to invoke external tools (function calling) based on their assessment of the user's intent. This capability turns standard LLM text generation into an active agent empowered to interact with live infrastructure: querying databases, triggering deployments, manipulating UI components, or reading/writing files. Instead of a static conversational interface, the model can 'decide' when a specific action is required, extract parameters from its context, and request the host runtime to execute that function, incorporating the result seamlessly back into the response generation loop. This interaction cycle facilitates building highly dynamic applications like AI-powered CMS plugins, autonomous refactoring agents, or complex administrative dashboards. By rigorously defining function signatures as schemas, developers provide clearly constrained access paths, ensuring the model operates predictably within safety guardrails while unlocking the full capability of the application's backend services through intelligent, intent-based action. Successfully orchestrating these tool calls is the cornerstone of building genuinely 'agentic' software rather than just another chatbot interface.

studentPrompts: How do defined function schemas enable the model to request concrete actions safely?
Describe a use case where tool calling transforms the interaction from passive generation to proactive action.
What are the benefits of using the official SDK instead of raw REST calls?
